sections.text: Welcome back. Algebra One, Module Six — linear inequalities. Equations give you one answer. Inequalities give you a whole range. Today we learn how to solve them, and the one rule that trips everyone up.

Picture this. You walk into a cookie shop with twenty dollars. Cookies are three dollars each. You also want one brownie for five dollars. How many cookies can you buy? Notice — the question isn't how many cookies do you have to buy. It's how many cookies can you buy. That's not an equation, that's an inequality. Three x plus five is less than or equal to twenty. Equations have one answer. Inequalities have a range.

Three things today. One — the four inequality symbols and what they actually mean. Two — solving inequalities, which works exactly like equations except for one twist. Three — graphing the answer on a number line, and using inequalities to solve real word problems.

Four symbols. Less than — strict, the answer is smaller, endpoint not included. Less than or equal to — the endpoint IS included. Greater than — strict, endpoint not included. Greater than or equal to — endpoint included. Memorize this. The line under the symbol means "or equal to," and that one extra line decides whether the boundary number itself is a valid answer.

Example one. x plus five is less than twelve. Same move as an equation — subtract five from both sides. That gives us x is less than seven. Done. Any number smaller than seven works. Six works. Zero works. Negative a hundred works. Seven itself does not, because the symbol is strict.

Example two. Three x plus two is greater than or equal to fourteen. Two steps, same as a two-step equation. Subtract two from both sides — three x is greater than or equal to twelve. Divide both sides by three — x is greater than or equal to four. Four works because the symbol includes equal.

Now the rule everyone forgets. Negative two x is greater than six. To isolate x, we divide both sides by negative two. If you do it normally you get x is greater than negative three — and that's wrong. The rule — whenever you multiply or divide both sides of an inequality by a NEGATIVE number, you must FLIP the inequality sign. So the correct answer is x is less than negative three. Test it. Pick x equals negative ten. Negative two times negative ten is twenty, and twenty is greater than six. It works. Flip when you divide by negative. No exceptions.

Your turn. Solve negative three x plus five is less than or equal to seventeen. Hint — subtract five first, then divide by negative three, and remember to FLIP. Pause now, solve it, press play when you're ready.

Time. Subtract five from both sides — negative three x is less than or equal to twelve. Now divide both sides by negative three, and because we're dividing by a negative we FLIP — x is greater than or equal to negative four. If you got x less than or equal to negative four, you forgot to flip. The answer is x is greater than or equal to negative four.

Graphing on a number line. Two rules. If the symbol is strict — less than or greater than — you draw an OPEN circle at the boundary, meaning that number is NOT included. If the symbol is inclusive — less than or equal, greater than or equal — you draw a FILLED circle, meaning that number IS included. Then draw an arrow in the direction of the solution. x less than seven — open circle at seven, arrow pointing left. x greater than or equal to four — filled circle at four, arrow pointing right.

Back to the cookie shop. Three x plus five is less than or equal to twenty, where x is the number of cookies. Subtract five — three x is less than or equal to fifteen. Divide by three — x is less than or equal to five. So you can buy at most five cookies. The inequality didn't just give you an answer, it gave you the whole range of possibilities — zero, one, two, three, four, or five cookies.

The number one mistake on this whole module. Look at negative two x is greater than six. On the left — the wrong answer. The student divided by negative two and forgot to flip, ending up with x is greater than negative three. On the right — the correct work. Divided by negative two AND flipped the sign, giving x is less than negative three. Always check — did I divide by a negative? If yes, flip.

Recap. Inequality symbols describe a range, not a single answer. Solving inequalities works just like solving equations — same steps, same moves. The one twist — when you multiply or divide both sides by a negative, FLIP the inequality. Graph the answer with an open circle for strict, a filled circle for inclusive, plus an arrow. Now go practice.

This video is the overview. To actually master inequalities, here's the rest. Read OpenStax chapter two point six on solving linear inequalities. Run the Khan Academy practice set on linear inequalities — fifteen problems, including the negative coefficient cases that catch most students. Do the assignment in your dashboard. Book an advisor check-in if anything still feels fuzzy. See you in Module Seven — introduction to functions.
